items.title: Compute Central
items.text:
Compute Central is a practical DevOps and cloud engineering knowledge base built around real infrastructure work.

Use it to learn, revise, and apply topics such as Linux, containers, Kubernetes, CI/CD, Terraform, Ansible, monitoring, troubleshooting, and system design. The focus is simple: explain concepts clearly, show how they work in practice, and connect them to day-to-day operations.

items.title: Start Here
items.text:
Kubernetes and OpenShift Learn core concepts, OpenShift operations, labs, troubleshooting, and quick-reference commands.

Automation Build repeatable workflows with Ansible, shell scripting, Terraform, and practical infrastructure examples.

CI/CD and Code Quality Set up Jenkins pipelines, SonarQube checks, and deployment workflows that are easier to review and operate.

Monitoring and SRE Use Prometheus, Grafana, Alertmanager, Loki, and troubleshooting patterns to understand system health.

items.title: What You Will Find
items.text:
• Step-by-step guides for common DevOps and SRE tasks
  • Kubernetes, OpenShift, Docker, Terraform, Ansible, Jenkins, and SonarQube notes
  • Monitoring and troubleshooting workflows for production-style systems
  • Architecture and system design references for platform engineering
  • Scripts, examples, and checklists that are easy to adapt

items.title: Learning Paths
items.text: Goal Good starting point Learn containers and orchestration Docker guide, then Kubernetes fundamentals Practice Kubernetes locally Minikube lab, Docker Desktop lab, or Podman lab Automate server work Ansible overview, Ansible playbooks, and shell scripts Improve delivery pipelines Jenkins setup, SonarQube integration, and Kubernetes CI/CD Operate production-style systems Monitoring stack, Kubernetes troubleshooting, and system design

items.title: How to Use This Site
items.text:
Start with the topic you need, then follow the examples in a local or test environment before using them in production. Most pages are written to help you understand the reason behind each step, not just copy a command and move on.

Best way to learn

Read the short explanation first, run the example safely, then write down what changed and why it worked.

Production reminder

Always review commands, credentials, namespaces, and environment names before running anything against shared or production systems.

items.title: About Sameer Alam
items.text:
I’m Sameer Alam, a DevOps Engineer and SRE practitioner focused on reliable, automated, observable, and secure systems.

My work includes infrastructure design, deployment automation, monitoring, incident response, platform operations, and simplifying complex workflows for teams.

I started documenting my learning in 2016. Compute Central brings those notes, experiments, and real-world lessons into one organized place.

items.title: AI Engineering Guide for DevOps and Platform Teams
items.text:
AI engineering is the practice of building systems that use models, data, retrieval, tools, and application logic to solve real problems. Modern AI is no longer just about prompting a model. In production systems, it usually involves multiple layers working together:

  • A model to generate or classify output
  • Retrieval to bring in relevant knowledge
  • Tools to take actions
  • Guardrails to reduce mistakes
  • Evaluation to measure quality over time

This guide gives a practical overview of the major concepts behind modern AI systems so the rest of the section makes more sense.
path: AI Engineering Guide for DevOps and Platform Teams

items.title: Why AI Feels Different Now
items.text:
Earlier software systems followed explicit instructions written by developers. Modern AI systems can generate outputs, reason across context, summarize large documents, search knowledge bases, and interact with external tools.

That shift matters because:

  • The output is often probabilistic, not fully deterministic
  • Quality depends on prompts, context, retrieval, and evaluation
  • Freshness often depends on external data, not only model training
  • Safety and correctness become engineering concerns, not just model concerns

A useful mental model

Think of AI engineering as a stack: model + context + retrieval + tools + evaluation + application logic
path: AI Engineering Guide for DevOps and Platform Teams

items.title: The Evolution of AI Systems
path: AI Engineering Guide for DevOps and Platform Teams

items.title: 1. Rule-Based Systems
items.text:
Early AI-style systems were mostly hardcoded logic and expert rules.

Example:

if "refund" in user_input.lower():
    print("Show refund policy")

Strengths:

  • Easy to understand
  • Predictable behavior
  • Good for narrow workflows

Limitations:

  • Brittle when wording changes
  • Hard to scale across many cases
  • No real generalization

Rule-based systems still matter today for validation, safety checks, and business constraints around AI outputs.
path: AI Engineering Guide for DevOps and Platform Teams

items.title: 2. Machine Learning and Generative AI
items.text:
Modern AI systems learn patterns from data rather than relying only on hardcoded rules.

In the current wave of AI, the most visible category is Generative AI, especially large language models (LLMs). These models generate text, code, summaries, translations, and structured outputs by predicting the next token based on prior context.

Common use cases:

  • Chatbots
  • Content drafting
  • Code generation
  • Summarization
  • Classification and extraction

Strengths:

  • Flexible across many tasks
  • Handles natural language well
  • Works well with unstructured text

Limitations:

  • Can hallucinate facts
  • Can be inconsistent across runs
  • May not know recent or private information
  • Often needs strong prompting and evaluation

Generative AI is powerful but incomplete by itself

A model can sound confident without being correct. In production, teams usually combine models with retrieval, tools, and evaluation rather than relying on the model alone.
path: AI Engineering Guide for DevOps and Platform Teams

items.title: 3. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
items.text:
RAG improves AI responses by retrieving relevant external information and providing it to the model at runtime.

Instead of asking the model to answer from training data alone, a RAG system:

  1. Receives a user question
  2. Searches a knowledge source
  3. Selects relevant documents or chunks
  4. Sends them as context to the model
  5. Generates an answer grounded in those materials

This is useful when the answer depends on:

  • Company documentation
  • Product manuals
  • Internal policies
  • Support articles
  • Frequently updated information

Benefits:

  • Better factual grounding
  • Better access to private knowledge
  • More recent information than model training alone

Risks:

  • Poor retrieval can lead to poor answers
  • Bad chunking can hide important context
  • Irrelevant context can confuse the model

Example flow:

User question -> Retriever -> Relevant documents -> Model -> Grounded response

RAG is often the first serious step from demo AI toward production AI.
path: AI Engineering Guide for DevOps and Platform Teams

items.title: 4. Tool Use and Model Context Protocol (MCP)
items.text:
Many tasks require more than text generation. They need the AI system to take actions or read live data.

Examples:

  • Query a database
  • Read a file
  • Call an API
  • Send an email
  • Check a calendar
  • Run code

That is where tool use becomes important. The model decides when a tool is needed, the system executes it safely, and the result is returned to the model for the next step.

Model Context Protocol (MCP) is a standard way to connect models to external tools and context providers in a structured manner.

You can think of it as:

Model <-> MCP <-> Tools / Data Sources / Apps

Why this matters:

  • It makes integrations more standardized
  • It separates model logic from tool wiring
  • It supports safer and more controlled external actions

Without tools, a model can only talk. With tools, it can actually help complete tasks.
path: AI Engineering Guide for DevOps and Platform Teams

items.title: 5. Agentic AI
items.text:
AI agents go beyond a single prompt-response interaction. They work toward a goal through repeated decision-making.

A typical agent loop looks like this:

  1. Understand the goal
  2. Decide the next step
  3. Use a tool or retrieve information
  4. Observe the result
  5. Adjust the plan
  6. Continue until the task is completed

Examples:

  • Research and summarize competitors
  • Triage support tickets
  • Investigate incidents from logs and dashboards
  • Generate a report from multiple sources
  • Perform multi-step coding or operational tasks

Strengths:

  • Can handle multi-step tasks
  • Can combine reasoning, retrieval, and tools
  • Can adapt when intermediate results change

Risks:

  • More moving parts means more failure modes
  • Tool misuse can create bad side effects
  • Longer loops may drift or waste tokens
  • Evaluation becomes harder than simple prompt testing

Agents increase capability and complexity together

Agents are not just bigger chatbots. They require careful design around permissions, monitoring, validation, and failure handling.
path: AI Engineering Guide for DevOps and Platform Teams

items.title: How the Pieces Fit Together
items.text:
A useful way to think about the progression is:

  • Rule-based systems decide with explicit logic
  • Generative AI produces flexible natural-language outputs
  • RAG adds relevant knowledge at runtime
  • Tool use and MCP let systems interact with the outside world
  • Agents coordinate all of the above across multiple steps

In practice, many real systems combine several of these patterns at once.

Example:

  • A support assistant uses an LLM
  • RAG retrieves policy documents
  • Tools fetch ticket details from a helpdesk system
  • An agent decides whether to summarize, escalate, or draft a reply

That is much closer to modern AI engineering than a single prompt in a chat box.
path: AI Engineering Guide for DevOps and Platform Teams

items.title: Common AI System Patterns
path: AI Engineering Guide for DevOps and Platform Teams

items.title: Prompt-only assistant
items.text:
Best for:

  • Brainstorming
  • Drafting
  • General Q&A

Weakness:

  • Limited freshness and grounding
path: AI Engineering Guide for DevOps and Platform Teams

items.title: RAG assistant
items.text:
Best for:

  • Internal knowledge search
  • Documentation Q&A
  • Support systems

Weakness:

  • Quality depends heavily on retrieval design
path: AI Engineering Guide for DevOps and Platform Teams

items.title: Tool-using assistant
items.text:
Best for:

  • Operational workflows
  • Productivity automation
  • Business system integrations

Weakness:

  • Requires stronger safety controls
path: AI Engineering Guide for DevOps and Platform Teams

items.title: Agentic workflow
items.text:
Best for:

  • Multi-step goals
  • Research tasks
  • Long-running business processes

Weakness:

  • Harder to test, debug, and control
path: AI Engineering Guide for DevOps and Platform Teams

items.title: What Makes AI Systems Hard in Production
items.text:
A demo can look impressive quickly. Production systems are harder because they need:

  • Reliability
  • Permission boundaries
  • Monitoring and logging
  • Cost control
  • Response quality evaluation
  • Defenses against hallucination and unsafe actions
  • Better UX when the model is uncertain or fails

Teams often underestimate that the hard part is not only the model. It is the full system around the model.
path: AI Engineering Guide for DevOps and Platform Teams

items.title: Practical Risks to Watch
items.text:
• Hallucination: The model invents facts or sources
  • Stale knowledge: The model lacks current or internal data
  • Prompt injection: Untrusted content tries to manipulate instructions
  • Tool misuse: The model triggers the wrong action or unsafe sequence
  • Cost creep: Large prompts, long contexts, and repeated calls increase spend
  • Evaluation gaps: Systems seem good in demos but fail on real edge cases
path: AI Engineering Guide for DevOps and Platform Teams

items.title: Good Engineering Habits for AI Teams
items.text:
• Start with a narrow, well-defined use case
  • Measure quality with real evaluation datasets
  • Log prompts, retrieval results, tool calls, and outcomes
  • Use retrieval when freshness or private knowledge matters
  • Add approval steps for sensitive actions
  • Prefer least-privilege tool access
  • Design graceful fallback behavior when the model is uncertain
  • Re-evaluate regularly as prompts, models, and data change
path: AI Engineering Guide for DevOps and Platform Teams

items.title: Where to Go Next
items.text:
This overview connects to the deeper topics in this section:

  • AI Agents
  • AI Model Evaluation

If you are new to this space, a helpful learning sequence is:

  1. Understand the difference between prompts, retrieval, tools, and agents
  2. Learn how agent systems make decisions across multiple steps
  3. Learn how evaluation works so quality can be measured instead of guessed

That combination gives you a much stronger foundation for building AI systems that are useful, reliable, and maintainable.
path: AI Engineering Guide for DevOps and Platform Teams

items.title: AI Agents for Automation Workflows
items.text:
AI Agents are systems that go beyond generating text. Instead of only predicting the next token, they take actions, make decisions, and complete tasks step by step to achieve a goal.

This is the key shift:

  • Generative AI → predicts next word
  • AI Agents → decide next action

items.title: Generative AI (Next Token Prediction)
items.text:
Generative AI models (LLMs) work by predicting the next token in a sequence.

Example:

Input: "The capital of France is"
Output: "Paris"

How it works:

  1. Break input into tokens
  2. Predict probability of next token
  3. Select most likely token
  4. Repeat until response is complete

Limitations:

  • No real goal awareness
  • Cannot take actions
  • Stateless (each prompt is isolated)
  • Can hallucinate

items.title: AI Agents (Step-by-Step Decision Making)
items.text:
AI Agents operate in a loop where they:

  1. Understand a goal
  2. Plan steps
  3. Take actions (tools/APIs)
  4. Observe results
  5. Adjust and repeat

Example goal:

"Find the best laptop under $1000 and summarize top 3 options"

Agent behavior:

  • Search web
  • Compare products
  • Summarize results
  • Deliver final answer

items.title: Core Difference
items.text: Aspect Generative AI AI Agents Core Function Next token prediction Action selection Goal Awareness No Yes Memory Limited Can maintain state Tool Usage No Yes Reliability Lower Higher (with iteration)

items.title: Agent Loop
items.text:
AI Agents follow a reasoning loop:

Goal → Plan → Act → Observe → Reflect → Repeat

This loop allows agents to improve results over time instead of generating a single response.

items.title: How Agents Use LLMs
items.text:
Important:

Agents still use LLMs internally.

But instead of using them once, they use them repeatedly to:

  • Decide next step
  • Choose tools
  • Evaluate results

LLM becomes the brain, agent becomes the system.

items.title: Example: Simple Agent
items.text:
def agent(task):
    if "weather" in task:
        return "Calling weather API..."
    elif "news" in task:
        return "Fetching latest news..."
    else:
        return "Planning next step..."

print(agent("weather today"))

items.title: Real-World Use Cases
items.text:
• Research assistants
  • Coding agents
  • Workflow automation
  • Customer support automation
  • Data analysis pipelines

items.title: Why Agents Are More Reliable
items.text:
Generative AI:

  • Produces one-shot answers
  • No verification
  • Can hallucinate

Agents:

  • Break problems into steps
  • Validate intermediate results
  • Retry if needed
  • Use external data sources

This makes agents better for real-world applications.

items.title: Agent + RAG + Tools
items.text:
Modern agents combine multiple systems:

id="p9d3sa" User Request ↓ Agent ↓ LLM (reasoning) ↓ RAG (knowledge retrieval) ↓ Tools / APIs ↓ Final Output

items.title: Types of Agents
items.text:
• Reactive Agents → respond immediately
  • Planning Agents → create multi-step plans
  • Tool-Using Agents → interact with APIs
  • Autonomous Agents → operate independently

items.title: Common Frameworks
items.text:
• LangChain Agents
  • AutoGen
  • CrewAI
  • Semantic Kernel

items.title: AI Model Evaluation for Reliable AI Systems
items.text:
AI model evaluation is the process of measuring how well a model performs against defined objectives. It ensures reliability, accuracy, and usefulness before deploying models in real-world systems.

Evaluation becomes more complex with modern systems like LLMs and RAG because outputs are not always deterministic.
path: AI Model Evaluation for Reliable AI Systems

items.title: Why Evaluation Matters
items.text:
• Ensures model correctness
  • Detects hallucinations
  • Measures performance improvements
  • Validates production readiness

Without evaluation, AI systems can produce misleading or harmful results.
path: AI Model Evaluation for Reliable AI Systems

items.title: Types of Evaluation
path: AI Model Evaluation for Reliable AI Systems

items.title: 1. Quantitative Evaluation
items.text:
Uses numerical metrics.

  • Accuracy
  • Precision
  • Recall
  • F1 Score

Best for:

  • Classification
  • Structured prediction tasks
path: AI Model Evaluation for Reliable AI Systems

items.title: 2. Qualitative Evaluation
items.text:
Human judgment-based.

  • Response quality
  • Relevance
  • Clarity
  • Helpfulness

Best for:

  • Chatbots
  • LLM outputs
path: AI Model Evaluation for Reliable AI Systems

items.title: 3. Benchmark Evaluation
items.text:
Compare models using standard datasets.

Examples:

  • GLUE
  • SuperGLUE
  • MMLU
path: AI Model Evaluation for Reliable AI Systems

items.title: Key Metrics Explained
path: AI Model Evaluation for Reliable AI Systems

items.title: Accuracy
items.text: Percentage of correct predictions.
path: AI Model Evaluation for Reliable AI Systems

items.title: Precision
items.text: How many predicted positives are actually correct.
path: AI Model Evaluation for Reliable AI Systems

items.title: Recall
items.text: How many actual positives were captured.
path: AI Model Evaluation for Reliable AI Systems

items.title: F1 Score
items.text: Balance between precision and recall.
path: AI Model Evaluation for Reliable AI Systems

items.title: Evaluating LLMs
items.text:
LLMs require different strategies because:

  • Outputs are probabilistic
  • Multiple correct answers exist
  • Context matters
path: AI Model Evaluation for Reliable AI Systems

items.title: Common Approaches
items.text:
• Human evaluation
  • Reference-based scoring
  • LLM-as-a-judge
path: AI Model Evaluation for Reliable AI Systems

items.title: LLM-as-a-Judge
items.text:
Use one model to evaluate another.

Example:

from ollama import generate

response = generate(
    model="llama3",
    prompt="""
    Evaluate the following answer based on relevance and correctness:

    Question: What is AI?
    Answer: AI is machines thinking like humans.

    Score from 1 to 10 with explanation.
    """
)

print(response["response"])
path: AI Model Evaluation for Reliable AI Systems

items.title: Ansible Automation Overview
items.text: Ansible is an automation tool for configuring servers, deploying applications, and running repeatable operational tasks. It is a good fit when you want simple, readable automation without installing agents on every Linux host.

items.title: What Ansible Is Good For
items.text:
• Provisioning packages and services
  • Managing configuration files
  • Running application deployments
  • Standardizing repeated operational work

items.title: Why Teams Like It
items.text:
• Agentless for most Linux environments
  • Uses SSH for remote access
  • Stores automation in YAML playbooks
  • Easy to read during reviews and troubleshooting

items.title: Key Building Blocks
items.text:
• Inventory: The list of hosts and groups you manage
  • Playbooks: YAML files that define tasks
  • Modules: Reusable units of work such as apt, copy, and service
  • Roles: A structured way to organize reusable automation

items.title: When to Use Ansible vs Terraform
items.text:
Use Ansible when the main job is configuring systems or running tasks on existing machines.

Use Terraform when the main job is creating infrastructure such as VPCs, subnets, load balancers, or cloud instances.

Many teams use both:

  • Terraform creates infrastructure
  • Ansible configures what runs on it

items.title: What Came Before Ansible
items.text:
Before Ansible became common, teams often relied on:

  • Shell scripts
  • Python scripts
  • Manual SSH sessions
  • Agent-based tools such as Puppet and Chef

Ansible became popular because it reduced complexity while still supporting real production work.

items.title: Quick Check
items.text:
ansible --version
ansible all -m ping -i inventory.ini

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
• Review Ansible core concepts
  • Practice with Ansible interview questions

items.title: Ansible Core Concepts Guide
items.text: This page covers the few Ansible ideas you should understand before writing playbooks.

items.title: Main Concepts
items.text:
1. Inventory The hosts and groups Ansible manages.
  2. Playbooks YAML files that describe the automation workflow.
  3. Modules Built-in actions for tasks such as installing packages, copying files, and managing services.
  4. Roles Reusable playbook structure for larger projects.

items.title: How These Pieces Work Together
items.text:
The usual flow is simple:

  1. Define servers in an inventory.
  2. Write a playbook with tasks.
  3. Use modules inside those tasks.
  4. Move repeated logic into roles as the project grows.

items.title: Practical Starting Point
items.text:
If you are new to Ansible, start with:

  • One inventory file
  • One small playbook
  • A few built-in modules such as ping, apt, copy, and service

items.title: Quick Check
items.text:
ansible all -m ping -i inventory.ini
ansible-playbook -i inventory.ini playbook.yml --check

items.title: Ansible Configuration Guide
items.text: Ansible configuration controls how commands behave, where inventory is loaded from, and how connections are handled. A project-level ansible.cfg is usually the cleanest approach.

items.title: Configuration Precedence
items.text:
When the same setting appears in multiple places, Ansible generally uses this order:

  1. Command-line options
  2. Environment variables
  3. ansible.cfg
  4. Built-in defaults

items.title: Common Config File Locations
items.text:
Ansible looks for configuration in this order:

  1. ANSIBLE_CONFIG
  2. ./ansible.cfg
  3. ~/.ansible.cfg
  4. /etc/ansible/ansible.cfg

items.title: Recommended Project Config
items.text:
Create ansible.cfg in the project root so the team shares the same behavior.

Example:

[defaults]
inventory = inventory.ini
host_key_checking = False
retry_files_enabled = False

items.title: Useful Settings
items.text:
• inventory: Default inventory file
  • host_key_checking: Useful in labs, but review carefully for production
  • retry_files_enabled: Disables retry file clutter
  • timeout: Controls SSH timeout behavior

items.title: Quick Check
items.text:
ansible-config dump --only-changed
ansible --version

items.title: Ansible Facts and Verbosity Guide
items.text: Facts help Ansible learn details about a host before running tasks. Verbosity helps you understand what Ansible is doing when something fails.

items.title: Gather Facts
items.text:
ansible all -m setup
ansible all -m setup -a "filter=ansible_python_version"

items.title: Why Facts Matter
items.text:
Facts are often used to:

  • Detect operating system family
  • Choose the right package manager
  • Read IP addresses, memory, and CPU details
  • Build conditional logic in playbooks

items.title: Verbosity Levels
items.text:
• -v: Basic extra output
  • -vv: More task and connection detail
  • -vvv: Deep debugging, including SSH and module behavior

items.title: Practical Tip
items.text: Use normal output first, then increase to -vv or -vvv only when needed. That keeps routine runs readable.

items.title: Quick Check
items.text:
ansible all -m setup -a "filter=ansible_distribution*"
ansible-playbook -i inventory.ini playbook.yml -vv

items.title: Ansible Installation Guide
items.text: This page gives the quickest practical ways to install Ansible on common platforms.

items.title: Linux with pip
items.text: pip install ansible

items.title: Ubuntu and Debian
items.text:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install ansible

items.title: macOS with Homebrew
items.text: brew install ansible

items.title: Windows
items.text: Use Ansible inside WSL with Ubuntu or Debian. That is the most practical and widely supported setup.

items.title: Verify the Installation
items.text: ansible --version

items.title: Practical Advice
items.text:
• Use one installation method per environment
  • Prefer package manager installs for simple lab systems
  • Prefer a controlled Python environment if you need version-specific behavior

Warning

Do not mix pip and OS package installs in the same environment unless you know exactly why.

items.title: Ansible Interview Questions for Automation Engineers
items.text: This page helps you prepare short, practical answers for Ansible interviews.
path: Ansible Interview Questions for Automation Engineers

items.title: How to Answer Well
items.text:
Give:

  1. A simple definition
  2. A practical use case
  3. One best practice or limitation
path: Ansible Interview Questions for Automation Engineers

items.title: Core Questions
path: Ansible Interview Questions for Automation Engineers

items.title: What is Ansible?
items.text: Ansible is an automation tool used for configuration management, application deployment, and operational tasks. It is popular because it is agentless for most Linux environments and uses readable YAML playbooks.
path: Ansible Interview Questions for Automation Engineers

items.title: What does agentless mean in Ansible?
items.text: It means Ansible usually connects over SSH instead of requiring a permanent agent on each target host. That keeps setup simpler in many environments.
path: Ansible Interview Questions for Automation Engineers

items.title: What is an inventory?
items.text: An inventory is the list of hosts and groups Ansible manages. It can be static, such as an .ini file, or dynamic from cloud or platform sources.
path: Ansible Interview Questions for Automation Engineers

items.title: What is a playbook?
items.text: A playbook is a YAML file that defines the tasks Ansible should run on one or more hosts.
path: Ansible Interview Questions for Automation Engineers

items.title: What are modules?
items.text: Modules are the units of work Ansible executes, such as installing packages, copying files, or managing services.
path: Ansible Interview Questions for Automation Engineers

items.title: What are roles?
items.text: Roles are a structured way to organize reusable automation. They help separate tasks, variables, templates, handlers, and defaults for larger projects.
path: Ansible Interview Questions for Automation Engineers

items.title: How are variables used in Ansible?
items.text: Variables make playbooks flexible. They help teams reuse the same logic across environments without hardcoding values into every task.
path: Ansible Interview Questions for Automation Engineers

items.title: What are facts in Ansible?
items.text: Facts are details Ansible gathers about a host before running tasks, such as OS family, interfaces, CPU, or memory. They are useful for conditional logic.
path: Ansible Interview Questions for Automation Engineers

items.title: When would you use Ansible instead of Terraform?
items.text: Use Ansible when the main job is configuring hosts or running tasks on existing systems. Use Terraform when the main job is provisioning infrastructure resources.
path: Ansible Interview Questions for Automation Engineers

items.title: Ansible Inventory Guide
items.text: Inventory tells Ansible which hosts to manage and how to group them. A clean inventory makes playbooks easier to reuse.

items.title: Static Inventory Example
items.text:
[webservers]
192.168.1.10
192.168.1.11

[databases]
db1.example.com

items.title: Common Inventory Tasks
items.text:
List the hosts Ansible sees:

ansible all --list-hosts -i inventory.ini

Ping all hosts:

ansible all -m ping -i inventory.ini

items.title: Python Interpreter Override
items.text:
This is useful when a server uses Python 3 in a non-default path.

[webservers]
192.168.1.10 ansible_python_interpreter=/usr/bin/python3

items.title: Practical Advice
items.text:
• Group hosts by role, not by random naming
  • Keep inventory readable and predictable
  • Use host variables only when a setting truly differs for one machine

items.title: Quick Check
items.text: ansible-inventory -i inventory.ini --graph

items.title: Ansible Playbooks: First Automation Workflow
items.text: Playbooks are where Ansible becomes useful in practice. They describe what should happen on a group of hosts in a readable YAML format.

items.title: Example Playbook
items.text:
---
- name: Install Apache on webservers
  hosts: webservers
  become: yes
  tasks:
    - name: Ensure Apache is installed
      apt:
        name: apache2
        state: present
      when: ansible_os_family == "Debian"

items.title: Run the Playbook
items.text: ansible-playbook -i hosts.ini playbook.yml

items.title: What This Playbook Does
items.text:
• Targets the webservers group
  • Uses privilege escalation with become
  • Installs Apache on Debian-based systems

items.title: Practical Advice
items.text:
• Start with one small, testable task
  • Use meaningful task names
  • Run with --check before applying changes when possible

items.title: Quick Check
items.text: ansible-playbook -i hosts.ini playbook.yml --check

items.title: Ansible Roles Guide
items.text: Roles help you organize automation into reusable, predictable pieces. They are the right next step once a playbook starts getting large.

items.title: Create a Role
items.text: ansible-galaxy role init my_role

items.title: Typical Role Structure
items.text:
tasks/
handlers/
templates/
files/
vars/
defaults/
meta/

items.title: Use a Role in a Playbook
items.text:
- hosts: webservers
  roles:
    - my_role

items.title: When to Use Roles
items.text:
Roles are useful when:

  • The same tasks are reused across playbooks
  • You want clearer structure for a team
  • Variables, templates, and handlers belong together

items.title: Quick Check
items.text: ansible-galaxy role init nginx_role

items.title: Ansible Variables Guide
items.text: Variables help you keep playbooks flexible instead of hardcoding values for every host.

items.title: Group Variables
items.text:
Use group variables when the same value applies to many hosts.

http_port: 80
ntp_servers:
  - 0.pool.ntp.org
  - 1.pool.ntp.org

items.title: Host Variables
items.text:
Use host variables only when one server needs different values.

http_port: 8080

items.title: Practical Advice
items.text:
• Put shared values in group variables first
  • Keep host variables small and specific
  • Use clear names that match the service or purpose

items.title: Quick Check
items.text: ansible-inventory -i inventory.ini --host myserver

items.title: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced
items.text:
Docker helps you package applications and their dependencies into portable units called containers. Those containers run the same way across laptops, test servers, and production systems, which reduces "it works on my machine" problems and makes delivery more predictable.

What Docker gives you

  • Consistent runtime environments across development, testing, and production
  • Faster startup and lower overhead than traditional virtual machines
  • Repeatable builds through Dockerfile
  • Easy multi-service orchestration with docker compose
  • Better isolation for apps, tools, and dependencies
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Core Concepts
items.text:
Before jumping into commands, it helps to separate a few Docker terms:

  • Image: A read-only packaged blueprint for an application
  • Container: A running instance of an image
  • Dockerfile: A text file that defines how an image is built
  • Volume: Persistent storage managed by Docker
  • Network: A communication layer for containers
  • Registry: A place to store and distribute images, such as Docker Hub

Container vs virtual machine

A container shares the host operating system kernel, while a virtual machine includes a full guest OS. That is why containers are usually lighter and faster to start.
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Installation
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Linux
items.text:
On Ubuntu or Debian-based systems, install Docker Engine with:

sudo apt update
sudo apt install ca-certificates curl gnupg -y
sudo install -m 0755 -d /etc/apt/keyrings
curl -fsSL https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu/gpg | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.gpg
sudo chmod a+r /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.gpg

echo \
  "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/docker.gpg] https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu \
  $(. /etc/os-release && echo "$VERSION_CODENAME") stable" | \
  sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/docker.list > /dev/null

sudo apt update
sudo apt install docker-ce docker-ce-cli containerd.io docker-buildx-plugin docker-compose-plugin -y

Verify the installation:

docker --version
docker compose version
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: macOS
items.text: Install Docker Desktop from Docker's official website, move it to Applications, and complete the first-run setup.
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Windows
items.text:
Install Docker Desktop from Docker's official website. On Windows, Docker Desktop typically uses WSL 2 for Linux containers, so make sure WSL is enabled if prompted during setup.

Docker Desktop vs Docker Engine

  • Docker Desktop is commonly used on macOS and Windows and includes a GUI, Compose, and local Kubernetes support.
  • Docker Engine is the lightweight server-side runtime commonly used on Linux.
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Enable non-root Docker access on Linux
items.text:
If you want to run Docker without sudo:

sudo usermod -aG docker $USER
newgrp docker

Test with:

docker run hello-world
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Common Docker Commands
items.text: These are the commands you will use most often:
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Inspect Docker
items.text:
docker --version
docker info
docker help
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Work with images
items.text:
docker images
docker pull nginx:latest
docker build -t myapp:1.0 .
docker rmi myapp:1.0
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Work with containers
items.text:
docker ps
docker ps -a
docker run nginx
docker run -d -p 8080:80 --name web nginx
docker stop web
docker start web
docker restart web
docker rm web
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Inspect logs and processes
items.text:
docker logs web
docker logs -f web
docker exec -it web /bin/sh
docker top web
docker inspect web

Interactive debugging

Use docker exec -it <container> /bin/sh or /bin/bash to inspect a running container. Minimal images often include /bin/sh but not /bin/bash.
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Understanding docker run
items.text:
The docker run command creates and starts a container.

docker run [OPTIONS] IMAGE [COMMAND] [ARG...]

Example:

docker run -d --name mynginx -p 8080:80 nginx:latest

What this does:

  • -d runs the container in detached mode
  • --name mynginx assigns a friendly container name
  • -p 8080:80 maps host port 8080 to container port 80
  • nginx:latest is the image to start

Other useful flags:

  • -e KEY=value to pass environment variables
  • -v host_path:container_path to mount storage
  • --rm to remove the container automatically after it exits
  • --network to attach the container to a custom network
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Building Images with a Dockerfile
items.text:
A Dockerfile defines how Docker should build your image.

Example:

FROM python:3.12-slim

WORKDIR /app

COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

COPY . .

EXPOSE 5000

CMD ["python", "app.py"]

Build the image:

docker build -t flask-demo:1.0 .

Run it:

docker run -d -p 5000:5000 --name flask-demo flask-demo:1.0
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Important Dockerfile instructions
items.text:
• FROM sets the base image
  • WORKDIR sets the working directory inside the container
  • COPY copies files into the image
  • RUN executes commands during build
  • ENV defines environment variables
  • EXPOSE documents the port the app listens on
  • CMD sets the default runtime command

Keep images small

Use slim base images when practical, combine related steps, and avoid copying unnecessary files into the image.
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Add a .dockerignore
items.text:
Just like .gitignore, a .dockerignore file prevents unwanted files from being sent into the build context.

Example:

.git
node_modules
venv
__pycache__
*.log

This keeps builds faster and images cleaner.
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Volumes and Persistent Data
items.text:
Containers are ephemeral by design. If a container is removed, its internal writable layer is lost unless data is stored externally.

Create a managed volume:

docker volume create postgres_data

Use it with a container:

docker run -d \
  --name postgres \
  -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=secret \
  -v postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data \
  postgres:16

List volumes:

docker volume ls

Inspect a volume:

docker volume inspect postgres_data
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Docker Networking
items.text:
Docker can attach containers to networks so they can communicate securely and predictably.

Create a custom network:

docker network create app-network

Run containers on that network:

docker run -d --name db --network app-network postgres:16
docker run -d --name api --network app-network myapi:1.0

Now the api container can usually reach the database by the container name db.

List networks:

docker network ls

Inspect a network:

docker network inspect app-network
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Docker Compose
items.text:
When an application needs multiple services, docker compose is much easier than running many docker run commands manually.

Example compose.yaml:

services:
  web:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    depends_on:
      - redis

  redis:
    image: redis:7

Start the stack:

docker compose up

Start in detached mode:

docker compose up -d

Stop and remove services:

docker compose down

View status and logs:

docker compose ps
docker compose logs
docker compose logs -f web

Rebuild after code changes:

docker compose up -d --build

Prefer docker compose

Modern Docker installations use docker compose with a space. The older docker-compose command may still work on some systems, but the plugin-based docker compose form is now the standard.
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Registries and Image Sharing
items.text:
After building an image locally, you can push it to a registry so others or your deployment pipeline can pull it.

Log in:

docker login

Tag an image for Docker Hub:

docker tag myapp:1.0 your-dockerhub-user/myapp:1.0

Push it:

docker push your-dockerhub-user/myapp:1.0

Pull it on another machine:

docker pull your-dockerhub-user/myapp:1.0
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Practical Workflow
items.text:
A common day-to-day Docker workflow looks like this:

  1. Write or update the application
  2. Define the image in a Dockerfile
  3. Build the image with docker build
  4. Run and test it locally with docker run or docker compose
  5. Inspect logs and fix issues
  6. Tag the final image
  7. Push it to a registry
  8. Deploy it in staging or production
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Best Practices
items.text:
• Use specific image tags instead of relying only on latest
  • Keep images small and focused on a single responsibility
  • Prefer official or trusted base images
  • Store secrets outside images whenever possible
  • Use .dockerignore to reduce build context size
  • Avoid running as root inside containers when the image can be hardened
  • Persist important state with volumes, not container filesystems
  • Use docker compose for multi-container development environments

Containers are not full security boundaries

Docker improves isolation, but containers still share the host kernel. For sensitive workloads, combine Docker with OS hardening, image scanning, least privilege, and good secret management.
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Troubleshooting
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Container exits immediately
items.text:
Symptom:

  • The container starts and stops right away

Checks:

docker ps -a
docker logs <container>

Typical causes:

  • The main process finished successfully and exited
  • The startup command is incorrect
  • A required file or environment variable is missing
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Port already in use
items.text:
Symptom:

  • Docker reports that a host port is already allocated

Checks:

lsof -i :8080
docker ps

Fixes:

  • Stop the conflicting process
  • Change the host-side port mapping

Example:

docker run -p 8081:80 nginx
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Build fails
items.text:
Symptom:

  • docker build returns an error during image creation

Checks:

docker build -t myapp:1.0 .
docker build --no-cache -t myapp:1.0 .

Typical causes:

  • Syntax issues in the Dockerfile
  • Missing files in the build context
  • Dependency installation failures
  • Cached layers hiding recent changes
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Cannot remove image or container
items.text:
Symptom:

  • Docker says the object is still in use

Checks:

docker ps -a
docker images

Fixes:

docker stop <container>
docker rm <container>
docker rmi <image>

If needed, force removal:

docker rm -f <container>
docker rmi -f <image>
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Docker daemon is not running
items.text:
Symptom:

  • Commands fail with a message that Docker cannot connect to the daemon

Checks:

docker info

Fixes:

  • Start Docker Desktop on macOS or Windows
  • Start the Docker service on Linux:
sudo systemctl start docker
sudo systemctl enable docker
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Quick Reference
items.text:
docker pull nginx:latest
docker build -t myapp:1.0 .
docker run -d -p 8080:80 --name web nginx
docker exec -it web /bin/sh
docker logs -f web
docker compose up -d --build
docker compose ps
docker compose down
docker volume ls
docker network ls
docker system prune

Use cleanup carefully

docker system prune removes unused objects such as stopped containers and dangling images. Review what it will delete before running it on a machine you care about.
path: Docker Container Guide: From Basics to Advanced

items.title: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu
items.text:
Jenkins is an open-source automation server used to build, test, and deploy software. It is one of the most widely used CI/CD tools because it supports pipelines as code, integrates with a large plugin ecosystem, and can automate everything from simple shell scripts to full production delivery workflows.

What Jenkins is commonly used for

  • Continuous integration for application builds and tests
  • Continuous delivery and deployment pipelines
  • Scheduled automation jobs
  • Infrastructure and operations workflows
  • Integration with Git, Docker, Kubernetes, SonarQube, Slack, and cloud platforms
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Core Concepts
items.text:
Before installing Jenkins, it helps to understand a few common terms:

  • Controller: The main Jenkins server that manages jobs, pipelines, plugins, credentials, and connected agents
  • Agent: A worker machine or runtime where Jenkins executes jobs
  • Job: A configured task in Jenkins
  • Pipeline: A multi-stage workflow defined in the Jenkins UI or a Jenkinsfile
  • Stage: A logical section of a pipeline such as build, test, or deploy
  • Plugin: An extension that adds features and integrations

Why pipelines matter

Jenkins pipelines let you version your delivery process in code, review it in Git, and keep environments consistent across teams.
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
Before installing Jenkins on Ubuntu, make sure you have:

  • A supported Ubuntu system
  • sudo access
  • Internet access to download packages and plugins
  • At least 2 GB RAM for light usage
  • Java installed, because Jenkins runs on the JVM

You can confirm the OS version with:

lsb_release -a
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Install Jenkins on Ubuntu
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: 1. Update system packages
items.text:
sudo apt update
sudo apt upgrade -y
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: 2. Install Java
items.text:
Jenkins requires Java. On modern Ubuntu systems, OpenJDK 17 is a safe default for current Jenkins releases.

sudo apt install openjdk-17-jdk -y

Verify Java:

java -version
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: 3. Add the Jenkins repository
items.text:
Import the Jenkins repository key:

curl -fsSL https://pkg.jenkins.io/debian-stable/jenkins.io-2023.key | sudo tee \
  /usr/share/keyrings/jenkins-keyring.asc > /dev/null

Add the Jenkins package source:

echo deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/jenkins-keyring.asc] \
  https://pkg.jenkins.io/debian-stable binary/ | sudo tee \
  /etc/apt/sources.list.d/jenkins.list > /dev/null
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: 4. Install Jenkins
items.text:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install jenkins -y
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: 5. Start and enable Jenkins
items.text:
sudo systemctl enable jenkins
sudo systemctl start jenkins

Check service status:

sudo systemctl status jenkins

Default port

Jenkins listens on port 8080 by default.
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Access Jenkins for the First Time
items.text:
Open Jenkins in your browser:

http://localhost:8080

If Jenkins is running on a remote server, replace localhost with the server IP or DNS name.
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Unlock Jenkins
items.text:
Retrieve the initial administrator password:

sudo cat /var/lib/jenkins/secrets/initialAdminPassword

Paste that password into the Jenkins unlock screen.
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Install plugins
items.text:
After unlocking Jenkins, select Install suggested plugins for a good default setup.

This usually installs common plugins for:

  • Pipeline support
  • Git integration
  • Credentials handling
  • Build triggers
  • Basic UI and administrative features
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Create the first admin user
items.text: After plugin installation, Jenkins prompts you to create an administrator account. Use a strong password and store it securely.
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Important First Configuration
items.text: Once Jenkins is running, a few settings are worth reviewing early.
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Manage plugins
items.text:
Go to:

  • Manage Jenkins
  • Plugins

You can install tools such as:

  • Git plugin
  • Pipeline plugin
  • Docker Pipeline
  • Kubernetes plugin
  • SonarQube Scanner
  • Slack Notification plugin
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Configure tools
items.text:
Go to:

  • Manage Jenkins
  • Tools

Common tool configuration includes:

  • Git
  • JDK
  • Maven
  • Gradle
  • Node.js
  • SonarScanner
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Configure credentials
items.text:
Go to:

  • Manage Jenkins
  • Credentials

Store secrets such as:

  • GitHub personal access tokens
  • SSH private keys
  • Docker registry credentials
  • API tokens
  • Cloud provider keys

Do not hardcode secrets

Keep secrets in Jenkins Credentials or an external secret manager, not inside the pipeline script.
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Jenkins Job Types
items.text:
Jenkins supports multiple job styles:

  • Freestyle project: Good for simple scripted jobs
  • Pipeline: Best for modern CI/CD workflows
  • Multibranch Pipeline: Automatically discovers branches and pull requests
  • Folder: Helps organize jobs and permissions

For most teams, Pipeline or Multibranch Pipeline is the preferred approach.
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Create Your First Pipeline
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: 1. Create a new pipeline job
items.text:
In Jenkins:

  1. Click New Item
  2. Enter a name such as first-pipeline
  3. Select Pipeline
  4. Click OK
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: 2. Add a simple pipeline script
items.text:
In the Pipeline section, choose Pipeline script and paste:

pipeline {
    agent any

    stages {
        stage('Check System Details') {
            steps {
                sh '''
                    echo "System Information:"
                    echo "--------------------"
                    hostnamectl
                    echo
                    df -h
                    echo
                    free -h
                    echo
                    uname -a
                '''
            }
        }
    }
}

Save the job and click Build Now.
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: 3. Review the build
items.text:
To inspect the result:

  1. Open the job
  2. Click the latest build number
  3. Open Console Output

You should see the system information printed by the pipeline.
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Pipeline as Code with Jenkinsfile
items.text:
For real projects, store the pipeline in your repository instead of writing it only in the Jenkins UI.

Example Jenkinsfile:

pipeline {
    agent any

    environment {
        APP_NAME = 'demo-app'
    }

    stages {
        stage('Checkout') {
            steps {
                checkout scm
            }
        }

        stage('Build') {
            steps {
                sh 'echo Building ${APP_NAME}'
            }
        }

        stage('Test') {
            steps {
                sh 'echo Running tests'
            }
        }

        stage('Package') {
            steps {
                sh 'echo Packaging application'
            }
        }
    }

    post {
        success {
            echo 'Pipeline completed successfully'
        }
        failure {
            echo 'Pipeline failed'
        }
        always {
            cleanWs()
        }
    }
}

Benefits of using a Jenkinsfile:

  • Pipeline changes are version-controlled
  • Teams can review CI/CD changes through pull requests
  • The same pipeline logic is reproducible across environments
  • Multibranch pipelines can detect branch-specific changes automatically
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Declarative vs Scripted Pipeline
items.text:
Jenkins supports two main pipeline styles:

  • Declarative Pipeline: Cleaner and easier to maintain for most teams
  • Scripted Pipeline: More flexible but also more complex

This guide uses Declarative Pipeline because it is the better default for most CI/CD setups.
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Working with Git Repositories
items.text:
Most Jenkins pipelines start by pulling code from Git.

Typical setup:

  1. Create a job or multibranch pipeline
  2. Connect the Git repository URL
  3. Add repository credentials if needed
  4. Point Jenkins to the Jenkinsfile

Jenkins can integrate with:

  • GitHub
  • GitLab
  • Bitbucket
  • Self-hosted Git servers

Common webhook use cases:

  • Trigger a build on push
  • Trigger validation on pull requests
  • Run branch-specific pipelines automatically
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Using Credentials in a Pipeline
items.text:
Jenkins Credentials should be referenced securely from the pipeline.

Example with username and password credentials:

pipeline {
    agent any

    stages {
        stage('Use Credentials') {
            steps {
                withCredentials([usernamePassword(credentialsId: 'dockerhub-creds', usernameVariable: 'DOCKER_USER', passwordVariable: 'DOCKER_PASS')]) {
                    sh 'echo "Using credential for ${DOCKER_USER}"'
                }
            }
        }
    }
}

Example with secret text:

pipeline {
    agent any

    stages {
        stage('Token Example') {
            steps {
                withCredentials([string(credentialsId: 'api-token', variable: 'API_TOKEN')]) {
                    sh 'echo "Token is available for command use"'
                }
            }
        }
    }
}
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Running Jobs on Agents
items.text:
In larger Jenkins environments, builds should run on agents instead of overloading the controller.

Example:

pipeline {
    agent { label 'linux' }

    stages {
        stage('Build') {
            steps {
                sh 'echo Running on a Linux agent'
            }
        }
    }
}

This allows teams to:

  • Separate workloads by operating system or tooling
  • Scale build capacity horizontally
  • Run specialized jobs on Docker, Kubernetes, or dedicated worker nodes
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Practical CI/CD Flow
items.text:
A common Jenkins pipeline lifecycle looks like this:

  1. Pull source code from Git
  2. Install dependencies
  3. Run linting and tests
  4. Build the application artifact
  5. Run code quality or security scans
  6. Build and push a Docker image if needed
  7. Deploy to staging or production
  8. Send notifications and archive artifacts

Jenkins becomes especially powerful when combined with tools like Docker, SonarQube, Terraform, Ansible, and Kubernetes.
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Best Practices
items.text:
• Prefer Jenkinsfile over UI-only pipeline definitions
  • Use agents for builds instead of using the controller for everything
  • Store credentials securely in Jenkins Credentials
  • Organize jobs with folders and naming conventions
  • Pin and review plugins carefully
  • Back up Jenkins configuration and job definitions
  • Keep the controller lean and avoid unnecessary workloads on it
  • Use webhooks instead of frequent polling when possible
  • Add post-build cleanup to reduce workspace clutter

Keep Jenkins maintainable

Jenkins can become difficult to manage if plugins, jobs, and credentials grow without structure. Standard naming, folder organization, and pipeline templates help a lot.
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Troubleshooting
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Jenkins service is not starting
items.text:
Checks:

sudo systemctl status jenkins
sudo journalctl -u jenkins -n 100

Common causes:

  • Java is missing or incompatible
  • Port 8080 is already in use
  • System memory is too low
  • Package installation was incomplete
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Cannot access Jenkins in the browser
items.text:
Checks:

sudo ss -tulpn | grep 8080
sudo ufw status

Common causes:

  • Jenkins is not running
  • Firewall blocks port 8080
  • Reverse proxy or DNS is misconfigured
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Plugin installation fails
items.text:
Common causes:

  • No internet connectivity
  • Proxy restrictions
  • Update center issues
  • Version compatibility problems between Jenkins and plugins
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Git checkout fails
items.text:
Checks:

  • Confirm the repository URL
  • Confirm the configured credentials
  • Ensure the Git plugin is installed
  • Test SSH keys or access tokens outside Jenkins if needed
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Pipeline fails at shell step
items.text:
Common causes:

  • Missing tools on the agent
  • Wrong file permissions
  • Script path does not exist in the workspace
  • Environment variables are missing

Example check:

steps {
    sh 'pwd && ls -la'
}
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Jenkins home is filling up
items.text:
Common causes:

  • Old workspaces not cleaned up
  • Large archived artifacts
  • Logs growing over time
  • Too many retained builds

Useful actions:

  • Configure build retention
  • Clean workspaces after jobs
  • Archive only necessary artifacts
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Quick Reference
items.text:
sudo systemctl start jenkins
sudo systemctl enable jenkins
sudo systemctl status jenkins
sudo cat /var/lib/jenkins/secrets/initialAdminPassword
java -version
sudo journalctl -u jenkins -n 100
sudo ss -tulpn | grep 8080
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
Once the first pipeline is working, the next useful improvements are:

  • Connect Jenkins to a Git repository
  • Move the pipeline into a Jenkinsfile
  • Add build, test, and artifact stages
  • Store secrets in Jenkins Credentials
  • Integrate Docker, SonarQube, or Kubernetes
  • Set up webhooks for automatic pipeline triggers
path: Jenkins CI/CD Installation and First Pipeline on Ubuntu

items.title: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations
items.text:
Kubernetes is a container orchestration platform for deploying, scaling, and operating applications across a cluster of machines.

This guide is written for DevOps engineers, SREs, and learners who want a practical mental model first, then safe hands-on commands.

What this page covers

  • Why Kubernetes exists
  • The runtime and kernel concepts underneath it
  • Local environments for learning
  • Managed platforms for production
  • Core objects you will use every day

Read this as a map, not a certification cram sheet

Learn the flow first: container -> runtime -> node -> cluster -> workload -> service -> ingress -> observability.

Copy-safe guidance

Examples on this page avoid patterns that age badly in production, such as :latest image tags or long-lived credentials. Pin versions and adapt manifests to your own environment before deploying them.
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Kubernetes Section Map
items.text:
• Fundamentals
  • CI/CD Pipelines
  • Scripting and Automation
  • Troubleshooting
  • OpenShift Guide
  • Hands-On Scenarios
  • Docker Lab
  • Minikube Lab
  • Podman Lab
  • Quick Reference
  • Interview Handbook
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Why Kubernetes Exists
items.text:
Before containers became common, teams usually deployed applications directly to physical servers or virtual machines.

That model created recurring problems:

  • Dependency conflicts between applications on the same host
  • Inconsistent environments across development, test, and production
  • Slow scaling and manual recovery during failures
  • Operational drift caused by hand-managed servers

Containers improved packaging and consistency, but running a few containers manually is very different from operating hundreds of them across multiple nodes.

Kubernetes solves the cluster-level problems:

  • Scheduling workloads across machines
  • Restarting failed containers automatically
  • Scaling services up and down
  • Exposing applications over stable networking
  • Rolling out updates safely
  • Managing configuration, secrets, and storage

Short version

Docker popularized containers. Kubernetes operationalized them at scale.
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Core Building Blocks Under the Hood
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: OCI
items.text:
The Open Container Initiative defines standards so container images and runtimes can work across tools and platforms.

  • The image specification defines how images are packaged
  • The runtime specification defines how containers are started and managed
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: runc
items.text: runc is a low-level runtime that creates containers using Linux kernel primitives. Most teams do not use it directly every day, but it helps explain what higher-level tools are doing underneath.
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Namespaces
items.text:
Namespaces isolate processes so a container gets its own view of key system resources.

  • PID namespace: process IDs
  • Network namespace: interfaces, routes, ports
  • Mount namespace: filesystem view
  • User namespace: users and groups
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: cgroups
items.text:
Control groups limit and account for resource usage.

  • CPU shares and quotas
  • Memory limits
  • I/O limits
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: containerd
items.text:
containerd is a production-grade container runtime used by many Kubernetes environments. It sits above lower-level runtimes and handles image management, execution, and lifecycle tasks.

Mental model

Kubernetes does not run containers by itself. It relies on node-level runtimes, which rely on Linux isolation and resource-control features.
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: A Minimal runc Demo
items.text: This is a learning exercise, not a normal day-to-day Kubernetes workflow.
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Install runc
items.text:
Ubuntu/Debian

sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y runc

RHEL-compatible

sudo dnf install -y runc
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Create a demo root filesystem
items.text:
mkdir rootfs
docker export "$(docker create busybox:1.36)" | tar -C rootfs -xvf -
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Generate a default runtime spec and start a container
items.text:
runc spec
sudo runc run mycontainer
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Inspect the container from the host
items.text:
sudo runc list
sudo runc state mycontainer

Why this matters

This shows that containers are not magic. They are regular Linux processes started with isolation and resource controls.
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Choosing a Local Kubernetes Environment
items.text: Use local clusters for learning, testing manifests, and validating deployment flow before touching shared environments.
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Minikube
items.text:
Minikube is a lightweight local Kubernetes environment and a strong choice for learning fundamentals.

Best for

  • First Kubernetes labs
  • Testing manifests locally
  • Exploring core kubectl workflows

Typical requirements

  • 2+ CPUs
  • 4 GB RAM recommended
  • 20 GB free disk
  • Docker, Podman, or a supported hypervisor

Install kubectl

Linux

curl -LO "https://dl.k8s.io/release/$(curl -L -s https://dl.k8s.io/release/stable.txt)/bin/linux/amd64/kubectl"
chmod +x kubectl
sudo mv kubectl /usr/local/bin/

macOS

brew install kubectl

Install Minikube

Linux

curl -LO https://storage.googleapis.com/minikube/releases/latest/minikube-linux-amd64
sudo install minikube-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/minikube

macOS

brew install minikube

Windows users should use the official start guide: Minikube Start

Start and verify

minikube start --driver=docker
kubectl get nodes
kubectl get pods -A

Cleanup

minikube stop
minikube delete
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Rancher Desktop
items.text:
Rancher Desktop provides a local Kubernetes environment with a desktop UI and supports containerd or dockerd.

Best for

  • Developers who want a GUI
  • Local testing with k3s
  • Teams already using Rancher tooling

Verify

kubectl get namespaces
kubectl config current-context

Official site: Rancher Desktop
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Docker Desktop
items.text:
Docker Desktop can enable a local Kubernetes cluster on macOS and Windows.

Best for

  • Local app development
  • Teams already using Docker Desktop

Verify

kubectl cluster-info
kubectl get nodes

Local cluster guidance

Local Kubernetes is for learning and development. It is not a substitute for production-grade cluster design, backup, access control, or node lifecycle management.
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Optional: Kubernetes Dashboard for Local Labs
items.text:
Use the dashboard for learning, not as your primary operational interface in production.

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/dashboard/v2.7.0/aio/deploy/recommended.yaml
kubectl proxy

Access: Kubernetes Dashboard via Proxy

Production note

Prefer kubectl, GitOps workflows, audit logging, and RBAC-reviewed access over relying on a web UI for cluster administration.
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Hands-On Browser Labs
items.text:
• Play with Kubernetes for temporary browser-based clusters
  • Vendor sandboxes and training labs for guided scenarios

Browser labs are useful for experimentation, but they are ephemeral and usually not suitable for repeatable team workflows.
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Managed Kubernetes Platforms
items.text: Production teams often prefer managed control planes so they can spend less time on cluster plumbing and more time on workload reliability.
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Amazon EKS
items.text:
Amazon EKS is AWS's managed Kubernetes service.

Good fit when

  • Your workloads already run heavily on AWS
  • You need IAM, CloudWatch, ALB, and VPC integration
  • Your team wants a managed control plane

Example flow

aws configure

curl --location "https://github.com/weaveworks/eksctl/releases/latest/download/eksctl_$(uname -s)_amd64.tar.gz" | tar xz -C /tmp
sudo mv /tmp/eksctl /usr/local/bin

eksctl create cluster \
  --name my-cluster \
  --region us-west-2 \
  --nodegroup-name my-nodes \
  --node-type t3.medium \
  --nodes 2

kubectl get nodes

Operational strengths

  • AWS IAM and networking integration
  • Managed control plane
  • Add-on ecosystem for autoscaling, ingress, and observability
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: OpenShift
items.text:
OpenShift is a Kubernetes platform with additional developer, security, and operational tooling.

Good fit when

  • You want a more opinionated platform experience
  • You need enterprise policy and built-in developer workflows
  • Your organization already uses Red Hat tooling

Try it

  • Red Hat Developer Sandbox
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Other common managed platforms
items.text:
• GKE for strong Google Cloud integration
  • AKS for Azure-native operations
  • IBM Cloud Kubernetes Service for IBM ecosystem users

Platform selection rule of thumb

Choose the platform that best matches your identity model, networking constraints, operations maturity, and existing cloud footprint, not the one with the longest feature list.
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: A Small containerd Demo
items.text:
If you want to see how a runtime behaves outside Kubernetes, this is a simple lab:

sudo dnf install -y containerd
sudo systemctl enable --now containerd

sudo mkdir -p /etc/containerd
containerd config default | sudo tee /etc/containerd/config.toml
sudo systemctl restart containerd

sudo ctr image pull docker.io/library/alpine:3.20
export CTR_NAMESPACE=my-ns
sudo ctr --namespace "$CTR_NAMESPACE" run -t --rm docker.io/library/alpine:3.20 my-container sh

sudo ctr namespaces list

Pinned image tag

The example uses alpine:3.20 instead of alpine:latest so the behavior is more reproducible.
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Core Kubernetes Objects You Will Use Often
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Deployment
items.text:
A Deployment manages stateless application replicas and rolling updates.

kubectl create deployment my-app --image=nginx:1.27.0 --replicas=3
kubectl get deployment my-app
kubectl rollout status deployment/my-app
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Service
items.text:
A Service gives pods a stable network identity.

kubectl expose deployment my-app --type=NodePort --port=80 --target-port=80
kubectl get svc my-app
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Ingress
items.text:
Ingress routes HTTP and HTTPS traffic into the cluster. It requires an ingress controller such as NGINX Ingress Controller or Traefik.

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: my-ingress
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  rules:
    - host: myapp.local
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: my-app
                port:
                  number: 80
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: ConfigMap
items.text:
ConfigMaps store non-sensitive configuration.

kubectl create configmap my-config --from-literal=APP_MODE=demo
kubectl describe configmap my-config
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Secret
items.text:
Secrets store sensitive values, but you should still treat them carefully and prefer external secret-management patterns in production.

kubectl create secret generic my-secret --from-literal=password='change-me'
kubectl get secret my-secret

Important

Kubernetes Secrets are not a complete secret-management strategy by themselves. In production, combine them with encryption at rest, RBAC, and external secret stores where appropriate.
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: PersistentVolume and PersistentVolumeClaim
items.text:
Persistent storage is how workloads keep data beyond the life of an individual pod.

apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolume
metadata:
  name: my-pv
spec:
  capacity:
    storage: 1Gi
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  hostPath:
    path: /mnt/data
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: my-pvc
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi

Lab vs production

hostPath is acceptable for local experiments. Production clusters typically use cloud block storage, shared filesystems, or CSI-backed storage classes instead.
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Helm Basics
items.text:
Helm is a package manager for Kubernetes applications. It helps teams install, upgrade, and templatize common workloads.

Install Helm

curl https://raw.githubusercontent.com/helm/helm/main/scripts/get-helm-3 | bash
helm version

Add a repository and deploy a chart

helm repo add ingress-nginx https://kubernetes.github.io/ingress-nginx
helm repo update
helm install my-ingress ingress-nginx/ingress-nginx

When Helm helps

Helm is useful when you need reusable packaging, values-based configuration, and lifecycle management for third-party applications.
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: A Safe First Kubernetes Workflow
items.text:
If you are just starting, this sequence gives you the right habits:

  1. Start a local cluster with Minikube or Rancher Desktop.
  2. Confirm context with kubectl config current-context.
  3. Deploy a small pinned-image workload.
  4. Expose it with a Service.
  5. Watch rollout status and inspect pods.
  6. Delete the workload and repeat from a manifest.

Useful verification commands:

kubectl get nodes
kubectl get pods -A
kubectl describe deployment my-app
kubectl logs deployment/my-app
kubectl rollout status deployment/my-app
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
items.text:
• Using :latest tags in examples or production manifests
  • Editing live objects manually without a source-controlled manifest
  • Assuming Ingress works without installing an ingress controller
  • Treating local cluster behavior as proof that production will behave the same way
  • Storing sensitive values casually in manifests or shell history
  • Skipping rollout verification after deployment
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Conclusion
items.text:
Kubernetes is most useful when you understand both layers:

  • The foundation layer: OCI, runtimes, namespaces, cgroups, containerd
  • The platform layer: Deployments, Services, Ingress, storage, Helm, and managed clusters

Learn the local workflow first, then move to managed platforms with stronger operational practices around access, networking, observability, backup, and release safety.
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Further Learning
items.text:
• Kubernetes Documentation
  • Minikube Documentation
  • Rancher Desktop
  • Amazon EKS
  • OpenShift Developer Sandbox
path: Kubernetes and OpenShift: From Basics to Practical Operations

items.title: Component Details
items.text:
API Server (kube-apiserver)

  • Front-end for Kubernetes control plane
  • Exposes REST API
  • Validates and processes API requests
  • Only component that talks to etcd
  • Interview Tip: Explain how it handles authentication, authorization (RBAC), and admission control

etcd

  • Distributed key-value store
  • Stores all cluster data
  • Source of truth for cluster state
  • Interview Tip: Discuss backup strategies, HA setup, and disaster recovery

Scheduler (kube-scheduler)

  • Assigns pods to nodes
  • Considers resource requirements, constraints, affinity/anti-affinity
  • Interview Tip: Explain scheduling algorithms, node selection criteria

Controller Manager (kube-controller-manager)

  • Runs controller processes
  • Node Controller, Replication Controller, Endpoints Controller, Service Account Controller
  • Interview Tip: Discuss reconciliation loops and desired state management

kubelet

  • Agent running on each node
  • Ensures containers are running in pods
  • Reports node and pod status to API server
  • Interview Tip: Explain pod lifecycle management

kube-proxy

  • Network proxy on each node
  • Maintains network rules
  • Implements Service abstraction
  • Interview Tip: Discuss iptables vs IPVS modes

items.title: Pods
items.text:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: nginx-pod
  labels:
    app: nginx
    tier: frontend
spec:
  containers:
    - name: nginx
      image: nginx:1.21
      ports:
        - containerPort: 80
      resources:
        requests:
          memory: "64Mi"
          cpu: "250m"
        limits:
          memory: "128Mi"
          cpu: "500m"
      livenessProbe:
        httpGet:
          path: /healthz
          port: 80
        initialDelaySeconds: 3
        periodSeconds: 3
      readinessProbe:
        httpGet:
          path: /ready
          port: 80
        initialDelaySeconds: 5
        periodSeconds: 5

Key Concepts:

  • Smallest deployable unit
  • Can contain multiple containers (sidecar pattern)
  • Shared network namespace and storage volumes
  • Ephemeral by nature

Interview Questions to Prepare:

  • Why use init containers?
  • Explain pod lifecycle phases
  • What happens during pod termination?
  • How do you debug a CrashLoopBackOff?

items.title: Deployments
items.text:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: nginx-deployment
spec:
  replicas: 3
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1
      maxUnavailable: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: nginx
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: nginx
          image: nginx:1.21
          ports:
            - containerPort: 80

Key Features:

  • Declarative updates for Pods and ReplicaSets
  • Rolling updates and rollbacks
  • Scaling capabilities
  • Self-healing

Interview Topics:

  • Deployment strategies (RollingUpdate, Recreate, Blue-Green, Canary)
  • How to perform zero-downtime deployments
  • Rollback mechanisms
  • Update strategies and their trade-offs

items.title: Services
items.text:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: nginx-service
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: nginx
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 80
      targetPort: 80
  sessionAffinity: ClientIP

Service Types:

  1. ClusterIP (default) - Internal cluster access only
  2. NodePort - Exposes on each node's IP at a static port
  3. LoadBalancer - Cloud provider load balancer
  4. ExternalName - Maps to DNS name

Interview Focus:

  • Service discovery mechanisms
  • How kube-proxy implements services
  • Headless services for StatefulSets
  • Service mesh integration

items.title: ConfigMaps and Secrets
items.text:
# ConfigMap
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
data:
